renæus
    ntroductory ote to renæus gainst eresies
 a.d. –.] his history introduces us to the hurch in her estern outposts. e reach the
banks of the hone, where for nearly a century hristian missions have flourished. etween
arseilles and myrna there seems to have been a brisk trade, and olycarp had sent othinus into
eltic aul at an early date as its evangelist. e had fied his see at yons, when renæus joined
him as a presbyter, having been his fellow-pupil under olycarp. here, under the “good urelius,”
as he is miscalled (a.d. ), arose the terrible persecution which made “the martyrs of yons and
ienne” so memorable. t was during this persecution that renæus was sent to ome with letters
of remonstrance against the rising pestilence of heresy and he was probably the author of the
account of the sufferings of the martyrs which is appended to their testimony.   ut he had the
mortification of finding the ontanist heresy patronized by leutherus the ishop of ome and
there he met an old friend from the school of olycarp, who had embraced the alentinian heresy.
e cannot doubt that to this visit we owe the lifelong struggle of renæus against the heresies that
now came in, like locusts, to devour the harvests of the ospel. ut let it be noted here, that, so far
from being “the mother and mistress” of even the estern hurches, ome herself is a mission of
the reeks   outhern aul is evangelized from sia inor, and yons checks the heretical
tendencies of the ishop at ome. nte-icene hristianity, and indeed the hurch herself, appears
in reek costume which lasts through the synodical period and atin hristianity, when it begins
to appear, is frican, and not oman. t is strange that those who have recorded this great historical
fact have so little perceived its bearings upon oman pretensions in the iddle ges and modern
times.
eturning to yons, our author found that the venerable othinus had closed his holy career by
a martyr’s death and naturally renæus became his successor. hen the emissaries of heresy
followed him, and began to disseminate their licentious practices and foolish doctrines by the aid
of “silly women,” the great work of his life began. e condescended to study these diseases of the
human mind like a wise physician and, sickening as was the process of classifying and describing
them, he made this also his laborious task, that he might enable others to withstand and to overcome
them. he works he has left us are monuments of his fidelity to hrist, and to the charges of t.
aul, t. eter, and t. ude, whose solemn warnings now proved to be prophecies. o marvel that
   usebius, book v. to the twenty-seventh chapter, should be read as an introduction to this author.
   ilman,   ist. atin hristianity , b. i. pp. , , and the notes.

the great apostle, “night and day with tears,” had forewarned the churches of “the grievous wolves”
which were to make havoc of the fold.
f it shocks the young student of the virgin years of hristianity to find such a state of things,
let him reflect that it was all foretold by hrist himself, and demonstrates the malice and power of
 
 the adversary. “n enemy hath done this,” said the aster. he spirit that was then working “in
the children of disobedience,” now manifested itself. he awful visions of the pocalypse began
to be realized. t was now evident in what sense “the rince of peace” had pronounced is mission,
“not peace, but a sword.” n short, it became a conspicuous fact, that the hurch here on earth is
“militant” while, at the same time, there was seen to be a profound philosophy in the apostolic
comment,   “here must be also heresies among you, that they which are approved may be made
manifest.” n the divine economy of rovidence it was permitted that every form of heresy which
was ever to infest the hurch should now ehibit its essential principle, and attract the censures of
the faithful. hus testimony to primitive truth was secured and recorded the language of catholic
orthodoy was developed and defined, and landmarks of faith were set up for perpetual memorial
to all generations. t is a striking eample of this divine economy, that the see of ome was allowed
to ehibit its fallibility very conspicuously at this time, and not only to receive the rebukes of
renæus, but to accept them as wholesome and necessary so that the heresy of leutherus, and the
spirit of iotrephes in ictor, have enabled reformers ever since, and even in the darkest days of
pontifical despotism, to testify against the manifold errors patronized by ome. ilary and other
allicans have been strengthened by the eample of renæus, and by his faithful words of reproof
and ehortation, to resist ome, even down to our own times.
hat the intolerable absurdities of nosticism should have gained so many disciples, and proved
itself an adversary to be grappled with and not despised, throws light on the condition of the human
mind under heathenism, even when it professed “knowledge” and “philosophy.” he task of renæus
was twofold () to render it impossible for any one to confound nosticism with hristianity, and
() to make it impossible for such a monstrous system to survive, or ever to rise again. is task
was a nauseous one but never was the spirit enjoined by cripture more patiently ehibited, nor
with more entire success.   f ulian had found nosticism just made to his hand, and powerful
enough to suit his purposes, the whole history of his attempt to revive aganism would have been
widely different. renæus demonstrated its essential unity with the old mythology, and with heathen
systems of philosophy. f the fog and malaria that rose with the ay-star, and obscured it, were
speedily dispersed, our author is largely to be identified with the radiance which flowed from the
un of righteousness, and with the breath of the pirit that banished them for ever.
he piscopate of renæus was distinguished by labours, “in season and out of season,” for the
evangelization of outhern aul and he seems to have sent missionaries into other regions of what
we now call rance. n spite of aganism and heresy, he rendered yons a hristian city and

arcus seems to have retreated before his terrible castigation, taking himself off to regions beyond
the yrenees.   ut the pacific name he bears, was rendered yet more illustrious by his interposition
to compose the aster ontroversy, then threatening to impair, if not to destroy, the unity of the
hurch. he beautiful   concordat   between ast and est, in which olycarp and nicetus had left
the question, was now disturbed by ictor, ishop of ome, whose turbulent spirit would not accept
the compromise of his predecessor. renæus remonstrates with him in a catholic spirit, and overrules
his impetuous temper. t the ouncil of ice, the rule for the observance of aster was finally
settled by the whole hurch and the forbearing eample of renæus, no doubt contributed greatly
to this happy result. he blessed peacemaker survived this great triumph, for a short time only,
closing his life, like a true shepherd, with thousands of his flock, in the massacre (a.d. ) stimulated
by the wolfish mperor everus.
he ntroductory otice of the learned translators   is as follows—
 
 he work of renæus   gainst eresies   is one of the most precious remains of early hristian
antiquity. t is devoted, on the one hand, to an account and refutation of those multiform nostic
heresies which prevailed in the latter half of the second century and, on the other hand, to an
eposition and defence of the atholic faith.
n the prosecution of this plan, the author divides his work into five books. he first of these
contains a minute description of the tenets of the various heretical sects, with occasional brief
remarks in illustration of their absurdity, and in confirmation of the truth to which they were opposed.
n his second book, renæus proceeds to a more complete demolition of those heresies which he
has already eplained, and argues at great length against them, on grounds principally of reason.
he three remaining books set forth more directly the true doctrines of revelation, as being in utter
antagonism to the views held by the nostic teachers. n the course of this argument, many passages
of cripture are quoted and commented on many interesting statements are made, bearing on the
rule of faith and much important light is shed on the doctrines, held, as well as the practices
observed, by the hurch of the second century.
t may be made matter of regret, that so large a portion of the work of renæus is given to an
eposition of the manifold nostic speculations. othing more absurd than these has probably ever
been imagined by rational beings. ome ingenious and learned men have indeed endeavoured to
reconcile the wild theories of these heretics with the principles of reason but, as ishop aye
remarks ( ccl. ist. of the econd and hird enturies , p. ), “a more arduous or unpromising
   n the authority of t. erome. ee uett é e,   e l’ é glise de rance , vol. . p. .
   he first two books of renæus   gainst eresies   have been translated by r. oberts. he groundwork of the translation
of the third book, and that portion of the fourth book which is continued in this volume, has been furnished by the ev. . .
ambaut. n attempt has been made, in rendering this important author into nglish, to adhere as closely as possible to the
original. t would have been far easier to give a loose and flowing translation of the obscure and involved sentences of renæus
but the object has been studiously kept in view, to place the nglish reader, as much as possible, in the position of one who has
immediate access to the reek or atin tet.

undertaking cannot well be conceived.” he fundamental object of the nostic speculations was
doubtless to solve the two grand problems of all religious philosophy, viz., ow to account for the
eistence of evil and, ow to reconcile the finite with the infinite. ut these ancient theorists were
not more successful in grappling with such questions than have been their successors in modern
times. nd by giving loose reins to their imagination, they built up the most incongruous and
ridiculous systems while, by deserting the guidance of cripture they were betrayed into the most
pernicious and etravagant errors.
ccordingly, the patience of the reader is sorely tried, in following our author through those
mazes of absurdity which he treads, in eplaining and refuting these nostic speculations. his is
especially felt in the perusal of the first two books, which, as has been said, are principally devoted
to an eposition and subversion of the various heretical systems. ut the vagaries of the human
mind, however melancholy in themselves, are never altogether destitute of instruction. nd in
dealing with those set before us in this work, we have not only the satisfaction of becoming
acquainted with the currents of thought prevalent in these early times, but we obtain much valuable
information regarding the primitive hurch, which, had it not been for these heretical schemes,
might never have reached our day.
ot a little of what is contained in the following pages will seem almost unintelligible to the
nglish reader. nd it is scarcely more comprehensible to those who have pondered long on the
original. e have inserted brief notes of eplanation where these seemed specially necessary. ut
we have not thought it worth while to devote a great deal of space to the elucidation of those obscure
nostic views which, in so many varying forms, are set forth in this work. or the same reason,
we give here no account of the origin, history, and successive phases of nosticism. hose who
wish to know the views of the learned on these points, may consult the writings of eander, aur,
and others, among the ermans, or the lectures of r. urton in nglish while a succinct description
of the whole matter will be found in the “reliminary bservations on the nostic ystem,” prefied
to arvey’s edition of renæus.
he great work of renæus, now for the first time translated into nglish, is unfortunately no
longer etant in the original. t has come down to us only in an ancient atin version, with the
eception of the greater part of the first book, which has been preserved in the original reek,
 
 through means of copious quotations made by ippolytus and piphanius. he tet, both atin
and reek, is often most uncertain. nly three mss. of the work   gainst eresies   are at present
known to eist. thers, however, were used in the earliest printed editions put forth by rasmus.
nd as these codices were more ancient than any now available, it is greatly to be regretted that
they have disappeared or perished. ne of our difficulties throughout, has been to fi the readings
we should adopt, especially in the first book. arieties of reading, actual or conjectural, have been
noted only when some point of special importance seemed to be involved.
fter the tet has been settled, according to the best judgment which can be formed, the work
of translation remains and that is, in this case, a matter of no small difficulty. renæus, even in the
original reek, is often a very obscure writer. t times he epresses himself with remarkable

 hilip chaff . he postolic athers with ustin artyr and renaeus

clearness and terseness but, upon the whole, his style is very involved and proli. nd the atin
version adds to these difficulties of the original, by being itself of the most barbarous character. n
fact, it is often necessary to make a conjectural re-translation of it into reek, in order to obtain
some inkling of what the author wrote. odwell supposes this atin version to have been made
about the end of the fourth century but as ertullian seems to have used it, we must rather place
it in the beginning of the third. ts author is unknown, but he was certainly little qualified for his
task. e have endeavoured to give as close and accurate a translation of the work as possible, but
there are not a few passages in which a guess can only be made as to the probable meaning.
renæus had manifestly taken great pains to make himself acquainted with the various heretical
systems which he describes. is mode of eposing and refuting these is generally very effective.
t is plain that he possessed a good share of learning, and that he had a firm grasp of the doctrines
of cripture. ot unfrequently he indulges in a kind of sarcastic humour, while inveighing against
the folly and impiety of the heretics. ut at times he gives epression to very strange opinions. e
is, for eample, quite peculiar in imagining that our ord lived to be an   old   man, and that is public
ministry embraced at least   ten   years. ut though, on these and some other points, the judgment of
renæus is clearly at fault, his work contains a vast deal of sound and valuable eposition of cripture,
in opposition to the fanciful systems of interpretation which prevailed in his day.
e possess only very scanty accounts of the personal history of renæus. t has been generally
supposed that he was a native of myrna, or some neighbouring city, in sia inor. arvey,
however, thinks that he was probably born in yria, and removed in boyhood to myrna. e himself
tells us (iii. , ) that he was in early youth acquainted with olycarp, the illustrious bishop of that
city.  sort of clue is thus furnished as to the date of his birth. odwell supposes that he was born
so early as a.d. , but this is clearly a mistake and the general date assigned to his birth is
somewhere between a.d.  and a.d. .
t is certain that renæus was bishop of yons, in rance, during the latter quarter of the second
century. he eact period or circumstances of his ordination cannot be determined. usebius states
( ist. ccl. , v. ) that he was, while yet a presbyter, sent with a letter, from certain members of the
hurch of yons awaiting martyrdom, to leutherus, bishop of ome and that (v. ) he succeeded
othinus as bishop of yons, probably about a.d. . is great work   gainst eresies   was, we
learn, written during the episcopate of leutherus, that is, between a.d.  and a.d. , for ictor
succeeded to the bishopric of ome in a.d. . his new bishop of ome took very harsh measures
for enforcing uniformity throughout the hurch as to the observance of the paschal solemnities.
n account of the severity thus evinced, renæus addressed to him a letter (only a fragment of which
remains), warning him that if he persisted in the course on which he had entered, the effect would
be to rend the atholic hurch in pieces. his letter had the desired result and the question was
more temperately debated, until finally settled by the ouncil of ice.
 
 he full title of the principal work of renæus, as given by usebius ( ist. ccl. , v. ), and
indicated frequently by the author himself, was    efutation and ubversion of nowledge falsely
so called , but it is generally referred to under the shorter title,   gainst eresies . everal other

smaller treatises are ascribed to renæus viz.,   n pistle to lorinus , of which a small fragment
has been preserved by usebius a treatise   n the alentinian gdoad  a work called forth by the
paschal controversy, entitled   n chism , and another   n cience  all of which that remain will be
found in our net volume of his writings. renæus is supposed to have died about a.d.  but there
is probably no real ground for the statement of erome, repeated by subsequent writers, that he
suffered martyrdom, since neither ertullian nor usebius, nor other early authorities, make any
mention of such a fact.
s has been already stated, the first printed copy of our author was given to the world by
rasmus. his was in the year . etween that date and , a number of reprints were produced
in both folio and octavo. ll these contained merely the ancient barbarous atin version, and were
deficient towards the end by five entire chapters. hese latter were supplied by the edition of
euardent, rofessor of ivinity at aris, which was published in , and went through si
subsequent editions. reviously to this, however, another had been set forth by allasius, a minister
of eneva, which contained the first portions of the reek tet from piphanius. hen, in ,
came the edition of rabe, a learned russian, who had settled in ngland. t was published at
ford, and contained considerable additions to the reek tet, with fragments. en years after
this there appeared the important aris edition by the enedictine monk assuet. his was reprinted
at enice in the year , in two thin folio volumes, and again at aris in a large octavo, by the
bb é   igne, in .  erman edition was published by tieren in . n the year  there
was also brought out a ambridge edition, by the ev. igan arvey, in two octavo volumes. he
two principal features of this edition are the additions which have been made to the reek tet
from the recently discovered   hilosophoumena   of ippolytus and the further addition of thirty-two
fragments of a yriac version of the reek tet of renæus, culled from the itrian collection of
yriac mss. in the ritish useum. hese fragments are of considerable interest, and in some
instances rectify the readings of the barbarous atin version, where, without such aid, it would have
been unintelligible. he edition of arvey will be found constantly referred to in the notes appended
to our translation.

. nasmuch   as certain men have set the truth aside, and bring in lying words and vain
genealogies, which, as the apostle says,   “minister questions rather than godly edifying which is
in faith,” and by means of their craftily-constructed plausibilities draw away the minds of the
ineperienced and take them captive,  have felt constrained, my dear friend, to compose the
following treatise in order to epose and counteract their machinations.] hese men falsify the
oracles of od, and prove themselves evil interpreters of the good word of revelation. hey also
overthrow the faith of many, by drawing them away, under a pretence of superior] knowledge,
from im who rounded and adorned the universe as if, forsooth, they had something more ecellent
and sublime to reveal, than that od who created the heaven and the earth, and all things that are
therein. y means of specious and plausible words, they cunningly allure the simple-minded to
inquire into their system but they nevertheless clumsily destroy them, while they initiate them into
their blasphemous and impious opinions respecting the emiurge   and these simple ones are
unable, even in such a matter, to distinguish falsehood from truth.
. rror, indeed, is never set forth in its naked deformity, lest, being thus eposed, it should at
once be detected. ut it is craftily decked out in an attractive dress, so as, by its outward form, to
make it appear to the ineperienced (ridiculous as the epression may seem) more true than the
truth itself. ne   far superior to me has well said, in reference to this point, “ clever imitation
in glass casts contempt, as it were, on that precious jewel the emerald (which is most highly esteemed
by some), unless it come under the eye of one able to test and epose the counterfeit. r, again,
what ineperienced person can with ease detect the presence of brass when it has been mied up
with silver” est, therefore, through my neglect, some should be carried off, even as sheep are by
wolves, while they perceive not the true character of these men,—because they outwardly are
covered with sheep’s clothing (against whom the ord has enjoined   us to be on our guard), and
because their language resembles ours, while their sentiments are very different,— have deemed

it my duty (after reading some of the   ommentaries , as they call them, of the disciples of alentinus,
and after making myself acquainted with their tenets through personal intercourse with some of
them) to unfold to thee, my friend, these portentous and profound mysteries, which do not fall
within the range of every intellect, because all have not sufficiently purged   their brains.  do this,
in order that thou, obtaining an acquaintance with these things, mayest in turn eplain them to all
those with whom thou art connected, and ehort them to avoid such an abyss of madness and of
blasphemy against hrist.  intend, then, to the best of my ability, with brevity and clearness to set
 
 forth the opinions of those who are now promulgating heresy.  refer especially to the disciples of
tolemæus, whose school may be described as a bud from that of alentinus.  shall also endeavour,
according to my moderate ability, to furnish the means of overthrowing them, by showing how
absurd and inconsistent with the truth are their statements. ot that  am practised either in
composition or eloquence but my feeling of affection prompts me to make known to thee and all
thy companions those doctrines which have been kept in concealment until now, but which are at
last, through the goodness of od, brought to light. “or there is nothing hidden which shall not
be revealed, nor secret that shall not be made known.” 
. hou wilt not epect from me, who am resident among the eltæ,   and am accustomed for
the most part to use a barbarous dialect, any display of rhetoric, which  have never learned, or any
ecellence of composition, which  have never practised, or any beauty and persuasiveness of style,
to which  make no pretensions. ut thou wilt accept in a kindly spirit what  in a like spirit write
to thee simply, truthfully, and in my own homely way whilst thou thyself (as being more capable
than  am) wilt epand those ideas of which  send thee, as it were, only the seminal principles and
in the comprehensiveness of thy understanding, wilt develop to their full etent the points on which
 briefly touch, so as to set with power before thy companions those things which  have uttered in
weakness. n fine, as  (to gratify thy long-cherished desire for information regarding the tenets of
these persons) have spared no pains, not only to make these doctrines known to thee, but also to
furnish the means of showing their falsity so shalt thou, according to the grace given to thee by
the ord, prove an earnest and efficient minister to others, that men may no longer be drawn away
by the plausible system of these heretics, which  now proceed to describe.

hapter .—bsurd ideas of the disciples of alentinus as to the origin, name, order, and
conjugal productions of their fancied Æons, with the passages of cripture which they adapt
to their opinions.
 . hey maintain, then, that in the invisible and ineffable heights above there eists a certain
perfect, pre-eistent Æon,   whom they call roarche, ropator, and ythus, and describe as being
invisible and incomprehensible. ternal and unbegotten, he remained throughout innumerable
cycles of ages in profound serenity and quiescence. here eisted along with him nnœa, whom
they also call haris and ige.   t last this ythus determined to send forth from himself the
beginning of all things, and deposited this production (which he had resolved to bring forth) in his
contemporary ige, even as seed is deposited in the womb. he then, having received this seed,
and becoming pregnant, gave birth to ous, who was both similar and equal to him who had
produced him, and was alone capable of comprehending his father’s greatness. his ous they call
also onogenes, and ather, and the eginning of all hings. long with him was also produced
letheia and these four constituted the first and first-begotten ythagorean etrad, which they also
denominate the root of all things. or there are first ythus and ige, and then ous and letheia.
nd onogenes, perceiving for what purpose he had been produced, also himself sent forth ogos
and oe, being the father of all those who were to come after him, and the beginning and fashioning
of the entire leroma. y the conjunction of ogos and oe were brought forth nthropos and
cclesia and thus was formed the first-begotten gdoad, the root and substance of all things, called
among them by four names, viz., ythus, and ous, and ogos, and nthropos. or each of these
is masculo-feminine, as follows ropator was united by a conjunction with his nnœa then
onogenes, that is ous, with letheia ogos with oe, and nthropos with cclesia.
. hese Æons having been produced for the glory of the ather, and wishing, by their own
efforts, to effect this object, sent forth emanations by means of conjunction. ogos and oe, after
producing nthropos and cclesia, sent forth other ten Æons, whose names are the following
ythius and iis, geratos and enosis, utophyes and edone, cinetos and yncrasis,
   his term Æon ( !"#$ ) seems to have been formed from the words   %&'   ($ ,   ever-eisting . “e may take   )"#$ , therefore,”
says arvey ( renæus , ci.), “in the alentinian acceptation of the word, to mean an emanation from the divine substance,
subsisting co-ordinately and co-eternally with the eity, the leroma still remaining one.”
   ige, however, was no true consort of ythus, who included in himself the idea of male and female, and was the one
cause of all things comp. ippolytus,   hilosop. , vi. . here seems to have been considerable disagreement among these
heretics as to the completion of the mystical number thirty. alentinus himself appears to have considered ythus as a monad,
and ige as a mere nonentity. he two latest Æons, hrist and the oly pirit, would then complete the number thirty. ut other
nostic teachers included both ythus and ige in that mystical number.

onogenes and acaria.   hese are the ten Æons whom they declare to have been produced by
ogos and oe. hey then add that nthropos himself, along with cclesia, produced twelve Æons,
to whom they give the following names aracletus and istis, atricos and lpis, etricos and
gape, inos and ynesis, cclesiasticus and acariotes, heletos and ophia.
